content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Many verbs are followed by a preposition + -ing form.
examples: Damien insisted **on going** to the party.
I'm looking forward **to meeting** your brother.

content.subtitle: Verb + object + -ing form
content.example: They caught him taking money from the till.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: The verbs feel, hear, see, notice, overhear and watch can also be followed by an object + the bare infinitive (without to).
examples: I saw Martha **cross** the road. (= I saw all of it.)
I saw Martha **crossing** the road. (= I saw part of it.)

content.subtitle: Different subjects
content.description: When the verb and the gerund refer to different subjects, we can use an object pronoun or a possessive pronoun to make it clear.
content.example: Do you mind **me/my** going out with your sister?

content.subtitle: Verbs followed by full infinitive
content.example: Can you afford to buy that car?

content.subtitle: Verbs followed by object + full infinitive
content.example: My sister advised me to tell Jim the truth.

content.subtitle: Verbs followed by object + bare infinitive
content.example: The teacher let the class leave early.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: In passive forms, make is followed by the full infinitive.
examples: Mum **made me apologise** to my sister.
I was **made to apologise** to my sister.

content.subtitle: Modal uses
content.description: The verbs dare and need can be used as modals, in which case they are followed by the bare infinitive.
examples: I don't **dare tell** Simone what happened.
You **needn't invite** Ralph if you'd rather not.

content.subtitle: Little or no change in meaning

categories.name: consider/imagine
categories.infinitive_example: I've always considered him to be a friend.

categories.name: forget
categories.infinitive: not do something you were planning to do
categories.infinitive_example: I forgot to ask Brian about the wedding.
categories.ing: not be able to remember a past event
categories.ing_example: I'll never forget asking Helen to marry me.

categories.name: go on
categories.infinitive: stop one action and start another
categories.infinitive_example: We chatted about football and then he went on to tell me about his divorce.
categories.ing_example: How can you go on living with Michael?

categories.name: like
categories.infinitive: be in the habit of doing; think it right to do
categories.infinitive_example: I like to eat with my family once a week.
categories.ing_example: I don't like being spoken to in such a rude manner.

categories.name: mean
categories.infinitive_example: I'm sure Rania didn't mean to upset you.
categories.ing_example: Being in love means never having to say you're sorry.

categories.name: regret
categories.infinitive: be sorry about giving someone bad news
categories.infinitive_example: We regret to inform you that the hotel is full.
categories.ing_example: Do you regret splitting up with Alec?

categories.name: remember
categories.infinitive: do something you are/were planning to do
categories.infinitive_example: Did you remember to order the flowers?
categories.ing_example: I don't remember asking for your opinion.

categories.name: stop
categories.infinitive: interrupt an action to do something else
categories.infinitive_example: Why didn't you stop to think before you acted?
categories.ing_example: Will you please just stop telling me what to do?

categories.name: try
categories.infinitive: make an effort to achieve something
categories.infinitive_example: Try not to forget her birthday.
categories.ing: do something as an experiment to solve a problem
categories.ing_example: You could try buying her some flowers.

content.subtitle: Preparatory it
content.description: With some verbs, such as find, think or consider, it is often possible to use it as a preparatory object.
content.example: I consider it incredible that James and Alice are still together.

content.subtitle: Form and use
content.description: The subjunctive is a verb form which does not take -s in the third person singular. It is possible to use the subjunctive in that clauses after words suggesting that something is necessary or preferable.
examples: The doctor suggested that Sam **take** some time off work.
It is very important that Greg **not know** about this.
It's absolutely essential that **I be** informed as soon as the President arrives.

content.subtitle: Alternative
content.example: The doctor suggested that Sam (should) take some time off work.

sections.title: A: Write the correct form of the verb
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: 1 The new school allows ...... (allow) students to choose when they want to take their holidays.
2 My parents can't afford ...... (get) a new car this year.
3 I don't recall ...... (have) any problems with the car before now.
4 It's no use ...... (sleep) on a mattress as old as that one.
5 You deserve ...... (know) the truth about what happened.
6 I can't help ...... (feel) a bit annoyed about what he said.
7 We're thinking of ...... (apply) to join the local tennis club.
8 I've arranged ...... (join) the gym, starting next week.
9 This room clearly needs ...... (need) decorating, doesn't it?
10 I was just about to leave when I happened ...... (bump) into an old friend.
11 My parents never let me ...... (help) with the cooking when I was young.
12 Might I suggest ...... (contact) your local Citizens Advice Bureau?

sections.title: B: Circle the correct verb form
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Kathy: Erm ... hello. You must be Preston. Preston: Yes, yes, I am. Kathy, is it? I have to admit (1) **to be / to being** a bit nervous. This is the first blind date I've been on. Kathy: Me too. I almost didn't come (2) **to see / seeing** you, actually. But then you just have to risk (3) **to meet / meeting** someone you don't like, don't you? Preston: Er... yeah. Do you mind (4) **to sit / sitting** by the window? Kathy: No, that's fine. It was my sister, Jane, who suggested (5) **to meet / meeting** you. Preston: Right. Did she happen (6) **to tell / telling** you anything about me? Kathy: She said you work together, but she refused (7) **to say / saying** anything else. Preston: Oh. Well, I've been looking forward (8) **to meet / to meeting** you. Erm ... do you feel like (9) **to order / ordering**? I'm starving. Kathy: Okay. I think I fancy (10) **to have / having** seafood. What about you? Preston: Seafood sounds good. Can you see our waiter?
items.answer: 1 to being, 2 to see, 3 meeting, 4 sitting, 5 meeting, 6 to tell, 7 to say, 8 to meeting, 9 ordering, 10 having

sections.title: C: Complete using the verbs in the box in the correct form
sections.instructions: Complete using the verbs in the box in the correct form. You may need to add a preposition. (allow, ask, buy, commit, promote, prove, put, recycle, rob, wait)

items.sentence: 1 How can you justify ...... so many lives at risk like that?
2 Everyone voted ...... Mr Greenwood to join the golf club.
3 The government have undertaken ...... more than a dozen helicopters from the US military.
4 People who have been accused ...... serious crimes are usually sent to prison to await their trial.
5 The man claimed ...... by two young men, but something about him made me suspicious.
6 We can't afford ...... for everyone to agree or we'll miss this opportunity.
7 We are trying to encourage all local people ...... more of their rubbish.
8 Why do you keep ...... me about what Susan said?
9 I challenge you ...... those accusations!
10 The assistant manager hoped ...... before too long.

sections.title: D: Correct the incorrect form
sections.instructions: One verb in each sentence is in the wrong form. Underline the incorrect form and rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: 1 You deserve to be given a medal for all the work you did when you volunteered to help Alan finish **to clear** out the attic.
2 The company has resolved to improve performance in the coming months and plans **resisting** being forced to close any offices.
3 Being married involves **to make** a lot of compromises and being prepared to learn to live with other people's faults.
4 Alec often appears to want to join in discussions but he needs to avoid **to be** so shy or he'll never manage to get his opinion across.
5 Health experts recommend taking up a sport, so consider joining a team or, if you can't face **to exercise** in front of so many people, plan to go running with a friend.
6 I detest being kept waiting, and it tends **happening** at this hotel more than any other, so stop giving me excuses.
7 What prompted me **applying** to join the police force was that my parents expected me to find a job which promised to offer security and good prospects.
8 Many young people yearn to get involved in making films, but few are prepared to endure **to work** for years to perfect their acting skills.
9 I dislike having to pretend **getting** on with someone when they don't attempt to get to know me.
10 If you are considering applying to become a student at Central University, we recommend you **to contact** the secretary, who needs **knowing** which course you are planning to follow.

sections.title: E: Complete with a pair of verbs
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence with a pair of verbs from the box in the correct form. (catch / wish, feel / follow, find / hide, glimpse / walk, hear / say, notice / try, observe / interact, see / come, smell / burn, watch / climb)

items.sentence: 1 I couldn't see clearly, but I thought I ...... Lily ...... past the window.
2 Did you ...... Nigel ...... that he was thinking of moving to France?
3 During the experiment, we ...... the animals ...... with each other.
4 Did you turn the oven off? I can ...... something ...... .
5 I looked everywhere for Johnnie and I finally ...... him ...... under the stairs.
6 We ...... the burglar ...... out of the window and then called the police.
7 I couldn't help ...... Simon ...... to get his car started, so I went to help him.
8 I thought I ...... someone ...... me, but when I turned round there was no one there.
9 Patrick ...... himself ...... he had never accepted the job in the first place.
10 I ...... Vanessa ...... towards me, so I quickly dived into the nearest shop to avoid her.

sections.title: F: Underline and correct the wrong verbs
sections.instructions: Underline twelve verbs in the wrong form and rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: Legal and social changes in Britain have led to many more people choosing **getting** divorced over the last three decades. The Divorce Reform Act (1969) let people **to get** divorced far more easily and, despite promising **staying** together for life, over 160,000 couples ended up **to get** divorced each year during the late 80s and early 90s, compared to just over 27,000 in 1961. Towards the end of the twentieth century, divorce rates tended **falling**, then started to rise again over the first years of the twenty-first century. When asked what motivated them **getting** divorced, people usually mention things like not **to feel** that the relationship was developing, or their partner making them **to feel** inadequate. Whatever causes people **separating**, it is often the children who have to learn **living** in a different family setting. They often resent **to have** to change their lives because the adults involved can't manage **resolving** their difficulties. Divorce has got easier legally, but it hasn't got any easier emotionally.
items.answer: 1 to get, 2 get, 3 to stay, 4 getting, 5 to fall, 6 to get, 7 feeling, 8 feel, 9 to separate, 10 to live, 11 having, 12 to resolve

sections.title: G: Complete with the correct form of the verb in bold
sections.instructions: Complete by writing the verb in bold in the correct form in each pair of sentences.

items.sentence: 1 I wish you'd stop **tell** ...... us you're going to get an A because it's not certain, you know.
2 Miss Edwards was explaining some grammar to us but she had to stop **tell** ...... a couple of the girls to be quiet.
3 When you're in town, remember **get** ...... some snacks for this evening.
4 I remember **get** ...... out of the car and I think I saw the motorbike, but everything after that is a blank.
5 We regret **announce** ...... that flight RA 721 has been cancelled.
6 I regret **announce** ...... that I was going to resign because I changed my mind soon after.
7 The lecturer ignored the interruption and went on **talk** ...... as if nothing had happened.
8 The lecturer described the social conditions in the 1930s and went on **talk** ...... about the effect of the war.
9 You're going to be angry, but I forgot **go** ...... to the bank to pay that cheque in.
10 Don't tell me you've forgotten **go** ...... to the Blue Mountains! It was only last summer.
11 The air conditioner wasn't working so I tried **change** ...... the batteries in the remote control, but it didn't make any difference.
12 There must be something wrong with the remote control because every time I try **change** ...... channels, nothing happens.
13 We like **have** ...... the car serviced at least once a year, just to be on the safe side.
14 No, don't! I really don't like **have** ...... my feet tickled like that!

sections.title: H: Complete the second sentence
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: 1 After working for six hours, I finally managed to finish the report. **in** I finally ...... the report after working for six hours.
2 Oscar is qualified to teach Russian because he has a degree in it. **to** Oscar's Russian degree ...... the language.
3 I couldn't believe it when they suggested that I should speak to the head teacher about the problem. **nominated** I couldn't believe it when they ...... to the head teacher about the problem.
4 I shared a bedroom with my brothers when I was young and I quite liked it. **like** I used ...... a bedroom with my brothers when I was young.
5 Tim resolved that he would stay out of trouble when he left prison. **to** On being released from prison, Tim resolved ...... trouble again.
items.answer: not to get into/never to get into

items.sentence: 6 Are you sorry that you invited Liam to join us on the trip? **regret** Do ...... Liam to join us on the trip?
7 The arrangement is that you are staying with Mr and Mrs Andrews. **for** We ...... with Mr and Mrs Andrews.
items.answer: have arranged for you to stay/ have made arrangements for you to stay

items.sentence: 8 What was your inspiration for creating such a fantastic character in your new novel? **to** What ...... such a fantastic character in your new novel?
1 When she suggested ...... over the weekend, I was so angry that I threatened to quit. A me to working B me to work C me work D me working
2 The manager requested that all staff ...... present at the meeting. A have been B be C are being D being
3 Didn't you ...... that Martha wasn't with Ashley? A think it strange B think strange C think strangely D think it strangely
4 It's very important that we ...... as soon as there's any change in the patient's condition. A be it notified B being notified C were notified D be notified
5 I feel ...... to inform the committee that a number of members are very unhappy with the decision. A my duty B this my duty C it my duty D that my duty
6 After what the manager said this morning, I didn't ...... him about the problems I've been having. A dare telling B dare it telling C dare tell D dare to telling
7 Don't you ...... that Veronica and George are still together? A incredibly find B find incredible C find incredibly D find it incredible
8 You shouldn't have let Kerry ...... without apologising for what you said to her. A leave B to leave C leaving D to leaving
9 It was a bit embarrassing when the teacher caught us ...... during the test. A to cheat B cheating C cheat D to cheating
10 It's not fair to make the dog ...... in the car on such a hot day. A to wait B waiting C wait D for waiting

sections.title: J: Write one word in each gap
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: 'You've been trying (1) ...... find that certain someone for far too long, Debbie,' they said. 'Can't you feel (2) ...... getting older? Don't you (3) ...... it strange to be 25 and not married yet?' they asked. They recommended (4) ...... to check out something called 'speed dating'. Two days later I found (5) ...... sitting in a club in central London with 100 other people with name badges on. The idea with speed dating is that you have three minutes to chat, then a bell rings and you change partners. You keep (6) ...... going like that all night, making a note of anybody you like. So, I sat there as 50 young men passed before my eyes. I didn't (7) ...... tell them that I worked for a young women's magazine, so I pretended to be a hairdresser. Everyone obviously found (8) ...... hard to know what to say – well, what do you say to sell yourself in three minutes? The Kevins, Jonathans and Michaels came and went, and I (9) ...... to feel like I couldn't (10) ...... to hear another potted biography. Speed dating might work for some, but I think I'll stick to traditional methods to find Mr Right.
items.answer: 1 to, 2 yourself, 3 find/think/consider, 4 me, 5 myself, 6 on, 7 dare, 8 it, 9 started/began, 10 stand/bear
